title: Firestarter - The Ran(King) of King - Part 1
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DxUH69hEi9k

Daniel (@dungeondive) launches an ambitious new series — The Ran(King) of King, reading and ranking every Stephen King book he can get his hands on — and opens with 1980's Firestarter. King, he says, is probably his favourite living author, but loving an author doesn't mean loving everything: he's candid about King's occasional clunky prose, dated racial stereotypes, and juvenile handling of sex.

Firestarter follows Charlie, a girl with dangerous pyrokinesis, and her father Andy, on the run from a shadowy government agency, "the Shop," after a college experiment gave her parents psychic powers. Daniel reads it as quintessentially anti-government, blue-collar-versus-the-man King, but finds it oddly low on stakes and menace, and structurally muddled (the threat isn't palpable until a third of the way in). His core problem: Charlie, an eight-year-old, isn't a very interesting character to anchor a novel — though he admits King made him genuinely *angry* at her for refusing to use her power. He praises King's gifts for setting and time ("meanwhiling") and for great lieutenant-villains (the creepy John Rainbird). Ultimately, though, it's just not memorable — lower-middle-tier King he'll never reread.

It becomes the series' first (and, for now, last-place) entry on his magnetic ranking board.

----

Is Firestarter underrated or rightly mid-tier King for you — and does a young child as protagonist help or hurt a novel?
